name: web-security-testing
description: Web application security testing workflow for OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities including injection, XSS, authentication flaws, and access control issues.
capabilities: Execute phase-based security testing workflow following OWASP methodology
Test for injection vulnerabilities including SQL, NoSQL, and command injection
Perform XSS testing across reflected, stored, and DOM-based variants
Evaluate authentication mechanisms and session management security
Check access control implementations and privilege escalation vectors
Verify security header configurations and deployment settings
author: sickn33
securitySummary: Static analysis flagged 33 potential issues (31 external_commands, 2 weak cryptographic algorithms). After evaluation, all findings are FALSE POSITIVES. The external_commands detections are markdown code formatting (backticks) used for skill references like @scanning-tools, not actual shell execution. The cryptographic flags are false positives from keywords in the OWASP checklist. This is a legitimate security testing workflow with no malicious code.
valueStatement: This workflow guides you through comprehensive security testing of web applications following the OWASP Top 10 methodology, from reconnaissance to reporting.
userTitle: Test Web Apps for OWASP Top 10 Vulnerabilities
limitations: Does not execute actual exploits - provides guidance only
Requires user to invoke referenced skills for specific testing
Does not include payload generation or fuzzing tools
No actual scanning or tool execution occurs within this skill

useCases.title: Comprehensive Security Assessment
useCases.description: Conduct a full security audit of a web application following structured OWASP Top 10 methodology with detailed phase-by-phase testing.

useCases.targetUser: Bug bounty hunters and ethical hackers
useCases.title: Bug Bounty Reconnaissance
useCases.description: Use the workflow for bug bounty hunting to systematically test target applications for vulnerabilities in a structured manner.

useCases.title: Security Validation
useCases.description: Validate that security controls are properly implemented in a web application before production deployment.

promptTemplates.title: Start Security Test
promptTemplates.prompt: Use @web-security-testing to test my web application for security vulnerabilities. Target: [URL]
promptTemplates.scenario: Begin a new security testing engagement

promptTemplates.title: Test for Injection
promptTemplates.prompt: We are in Phase 2 of @web-security-testing. Test for SQL injection on the login form at [URL] with parameter [param]

promptTemplates.title: XSS Assessment
promptTemplates.prompt: Following Phase 3 of @web-security-testing, test for XSS vulnerabilities in the comment section at [URL]
promptTemplates.scenario: Conduct XSS testing on specific feature

promptTemplates.title: Complete Security Report
promptTemplates.prompt: We have completed all phases of @web-security-testing. Generate a security report summarizing findings and remediation steps.

bestPractices: Always obtain proper authorization before testing any application
Follow the workflow phases in order for comprehensive coverage
Document all findings with proof of concept for each vulnerability
Invoke referenced skills for specialized testing in each phase
antiPatterns: Skipping phases - each phase builds on previous reconnaissance
Not documenting findings with reproduction steps
Ignoring low-severity findings without proper risk assessment

faq.question: Does this skill execute actual exploits?
faq.answer: No. This is a workflow guidance skill that provides testing methodology and prompts. It does not execute exploits or run tools directly.

faq.question: Do I need other skills to use this workflow?
faq.answer: Yes. This workflow references other skills like @scanning-tools, @sql-injection-testing, @xss-html-injection, and @broken-authentication for specific testing phases.

faq.question: Is this suitable for production testing?
faq.answer: Only with proper written authorization. Always ensure you have explicit permission before testing any web application.

faq.question: What OWASP categories are covered?
faq.answer: All OWASP Top 10 2021 categories are covered including injection, broken authentication, sensitive data exposure, XML external entities, broken access control, security misconfiguration, cross-site scripting, insecure deserialization, using vulnerable components, and insufficient logging.

faq.question: Can I customize this workflow?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow phases can be adapted based on your target application and scope. Add or modify phases as needed for your assessment.

faq.question: What output format should I use for reports?
faq.answer: Follow the reporting phase guidance to document vulnerabilities with severity, proof of concept, and remediation steps. Use industry standard formats.
